text: The surgeon releases the endopelvic fascia on the left side, working medially over the prostate using robotic instruments.
The surgeon grasps the veins over the prostate to gain access to the medial aspect of the lateral pelvic fascia or the prostatic fascia.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to retract and better expose the prostate while working posteriorly. Some bleeding is visible from the Santorini plexus veins during this maneuver.
The surgeon identifies the lateral pelvic fascia or prostatic fascia, focusing on the anatomical structures surrounding the prostate during the procedure.
The surgeon uses counter traction with the left hand to better identify the optimal nerve-sparing plane during the prostatectomy.
The surgeon continues dissection along the identified nerve-sparing plane, extending apically and posteriorly around the prostate to maximize exposure and preservation of surrounding structures.
The surgeon releases the endopelvic fascia on the right side, both medially and laterally, around the veins that course over the prostate.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to grasp the veins for retraction while the left hand assists with dissection and provides counter traction around the prostate.
The surgeon uses counter traction with the left hand to expose the prostatic fascia, facilitating dissection with scissors.
The surgeon drops the nerves laterally and continues dissection posteriorly along the back of the prostate, aiming to connect the dissection planes established by the right and left hands.
The surgeon uses a red vessel loop to pass from the right hand side to the left hand side, demonstrating the complete connection of the nerve sparing plane posteriorly along the prostate.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to grasp the bladder superiorly, facilitating posterior dissection aimed at targeting the vas deferens.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens and seminal vesicle, continuing the posterior dissection using spot cautery to carefully free the seminal vesicles from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon grasps and retracts the freed seminal vesicles superiorly, then incises Denonvilliers' fascia, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon creates a dissection plane just underneath the prostate, progressing apically until encountering the red vessel loop, which marks the nerve-sparing plane.
The surgeon dissects the plane laterally to form a thin prostatic pedicle, carefully separating the tissue along the prostate's lateral aspect.
The surgeon dissects the lateral bladder pedicles, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies blood vessels branching from the internal iliac artery, positioned laterally to the ureter.
The surgeon focuses on dissecting the posterior pedicles while preserving the surrounding nerves, ensuring careful manipulation to maintain nerve integrity.
The surgeon retracts the bladder superiorly and uses a vessel sealer to transect the lateral bladder pedicles, ensuring clear visualization of the pedicles during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm to retract tissues below the ureters, enhancing exposure and visibility to prevent positive surgical margins during the procedure.
The surgeon reaches the prostate pedicle and uses both ends of a red vessel loop to thin out the pedicle, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon retracts the red vessel loop to thin out the prostate pedicle, then applies a Weck clip to secure the pedicle.
The surgeon uses scissors to sharply transect the prostate pedicle, following previous ligation and thinning of the pedicle.
The surgeon assesses the thinned prostate pedicle, preparing to complete the ligation using one to two weck clips.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to ligate the lateral bladder pedicles on the right side, focusing on securing these structures during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon ligates the posterior bladder pedicles using a vessel sealer, while the fourth robotic arm retracts tissue to maintain a position underneath the ureter, ensuring clear surgical margins.
The surgeon reaches the prostate pedicle and uses the vessel loop to grasp and retract it, thinning out the pedicle to facilitate the application of a weck clip.
The surgeon applies one to two weck clips to the dissected prostate pedicle, ensuring it is sufficiently thinned out for effective clipping.
The surgeon has dropped the bladder and transected the urethra, revealing the prostate, which appears smooth and free of any nerve tissue.
The surgeon addresses minor bleeding at the apex bed by applying hemostatic agents, taking care to avoid cautery injury to the surrounding nerves.
The completed result of the prostatectomy is shown, highlighting the nerve-sparing approach with the prostate visibly freed from surrounding nerve tissue.
